reason: The item cleanly tests admissible inference boundaries from a mixed-success outcome (propagation suppression with a time-limited cooling failure), and the correct set requires careful separation of what the observations do and do not entail. Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible (mechanism substitution, regime-transfer, and overclaim), creating good expert discrimination, though some options invite mild interpretive latitude around what mass loss and voltage retention strictly prove.

The item cleanly targets boundary-aware engineering judgment: admissible conclusions must integrate onset-delay, combustion behavior, and pack-propagation evidence without overgeneralizing. Decoys are largely single-fault and plausible (overreach, metric primacy error, mechanism confusion), creating real expert discrimination, though A’s wording has a minor internal inconsistency that can introduce slight ambiguity. Overall, it strongly rewards multi-metric safety reasoning while penalizing common heuristic shortcuts.

The item tightly targets a boundary-aware engineering judgment: rejecting a single-metric (onset delay) substitution argument when another safety-critical property (immersion-fluid flammability/post-onset behavior) dominates pack hazard severity. It has strong discriminative power because a plausible rationale is given for the proposed change, but experts must explicitly articulate why that rationale fails at the system level. Minor risk remains that as a short-answer item, grading could be sensitive to wording and to how broadly respondents discuss pack-level transfer rather than the specific fluid-combustion mechanism.
